inline_comments.message: this sounds integral, so wondering why it's Double

maybe introduce a makeCopy(source, Class<Map> newClassType) ?
or addAll(Map<> target, Map<> source) ?

either of which return null if the source is null

or simply do inline check for null and then do: new HashMap<>(source)

could replace this whole block, including the "if" test, with a call to PfUtils.makeMap - would reduce the whole block to one statement.

similar comment for attributes and occurrences

that would require a minor tweak to makeMap (which should be done anyway :-) ) to take Function instead of UnaryFunction
I believe you can use a static object for all of these
remove this - no reason to convert to double only to auto-box back to Double when adding to the list
same comment as before for these blocks

use propertyEntry.set() instead of put()

same comment below

might as well add a "break" here - no point in duplicating the message

same comment for the other cases below
maybe use "xxx < 0.0 && xxx != unbound-value", to prevent things like -0.5
why not just call super.compareTo() and return the result if != 0?
could use makeMap, but the data structure is complicated enough that this is OK as is
do we not have a utility method for this?  if not, we probably should
this won't work as it doesn't modify the value in place
seem to be doing this regularly - maybe make a utility method
same comments as other classes: use makeCopy, makeMap, compareTo, add utility methods
same comments as other classes: use makeCopy, makeMap, compareTo, add utility methods
same comments as other classes: use makeCopy, makeMap, compareTo, add utility methods

I tried to get it to work as an integer but the serialization and deserialization of the yaml treats all numbers as double so it was better just to leave them as doubles the whole way through.

Also the fact that the string "unbounded" is a legal value for occurrences causes all sorts of problems, these TOSCA people certainly don't make it easy for people trying to work with the language.
Yes, I need to change all these in all classes.
oh, well let me fix the YamlJsonTranslator - already made a similar fix to the Json translators.
That's weird - I had the same issue in XACML PDP - but I thought Liam that you (we) fixed it somewhere.

the following should work:

  new YamlJsonTranslator(GsonMessageBodyHandler.configBuilder(new GsonBuilder()))

Apologies, rather than putting in a "break", I'm now printing the key of the value that's null, so, while there might still be multiple messages, now the user will see which keys have hull values. I thought this was more useful than bombing out on the first one and having the user come back and fix that only to have another one bomb out.
OK, I'll fix the rest of the comments and come back on this one.
@Jim, I copied this code from one of the other existing classes. Was it you wrote the mapMethod? Just wondering why its an issue to use it here?

in this case, makeCopy is appropriate as it does exactly what you want, it copies the map.  In addition, it is more efficient as it would only use assignments to copy the String values instead of invoking the mapping function.  makeMap() is OK as alternative, but then you'd want to replace String:new with an identity function, as there's no need to create another copy of the String when the original will work just fine.

For that matter, this would be fine, too:

  copy.attr == null ? null : new LinkedHashMap<>(copy.attr)

I think this should be:

  occurrences.size()

might have the same issue elsewhere
Yes,my change can cope with null values.

is this check really necessary?

same question on line 101
since JpaToscaProperty is mutable, shouldn't we make a copy of each property?
at one point, sonar complained that should just return the value here.  maybe it doesn't care anymore?
is sonar ok with println since it's in a junit?
Yes, because the semantics here is that if one of the colletions is null and the other is empty, then they are considered to be semantically equal.
There is some reason for not checking this that I can't remember now, I'll check.

I think that makeCopy() would have different behaviour, I think ti would result in a new map with references to the same objects whereas what I want is a new map with new instances of each of the objects.
ah, didn't realize that isEmpty() returns true if the collection is null
is this really needed?  seems like compareCollections() would work just fine
Both the key & value are Strings, and as Strings are immutable, it's ok to copy the references rather than creating new instances
We need this for maps because with the generics, it enforces the two maps to be of the same type.
done, removed, this was test code for me to check what the coders were doing.
I was just tweaking an existing test here, maybe we can come back and clean up these assertions in this test class in a later review?
nvm - a Map isn't a Collection, duh
